coredata.dc:title: Gelatin film cross-linked with laccase-catalyzed purple cauliflower extract and coated with Zn-carbon dots exhibits excellent physicochemical properties and functionality for intelligent and active packaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanyan
Jiang, Longwei
Liu, Tiantian
Li, Mei
Li, Ziao
Liu, Yingzhu
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of oxidized purple cauliflower extract (OPE) was conducted by laccase oxidation and served as a reinforcing agent for enhancing physicochemical properties of gelatin (Gel) films. Chitosan (CS) loaded with biowaste-derived Zn-carbon dots (Zn-CD) coating further enhanced the functionality and stability of Gel/OPE film. Structural characterization revealed that OPE and Gel form Schiff base bonds and hydrogen bonds. After the formation of CS/Zn-CD coating, hydrogen bonds formed between CS and Zn-CD, and CS and Gel were closely bonded through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. OPE crosslinking and CS/Zn-CD coating improve mechanical, barrier properties and water resistance of Gel films. CS/Zn-CD coating improved antibacterial and antioxidant ability and illumination stability of composite film, effectively reflected the freshness of salmon during storage. Hence, the functional Gel films developed in this study is hopeful candidate for intelligent and active food packaging application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gelatin film
Zn-carbon dots
Intelligent and active packaging
Purple cauliflower
Laccase

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra (a), XRD pattern (b) of films, XPS spectra (c), C 1s spectra (d–f), and N 1s spectra (g–i) of Gel, Gel/OPE4 and Gel/OPE8 films.
Surface (first and second rows) and cross-section (third and fourth rows) morphology of films, EDS results (fourth row), and EDS map-scanning of C, N, O, Zn (fifth row) of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8.
Apparent shear viscosity (a) of Gel/PE8 and Gel/OPE8 blends, TGA (b) of films, the schematic diagram (c) of film formation mechanism, WVP (d), OP (e) and WCA (f) of films.

The digital photos (a), color change under buffer solutions at pH 3–11 (b), light transmittance (c), DPPH radical scavenging activity (d), ABTS radical scavenging activity (e), antimicrobial activity (f) and the image of antimicrobial activity (g) of the films. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Application of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 on monitoring of salmon freshness. Apparent color change (a) and S value (b) of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 in monitoring salmon freshness, and pH, TVB-N change (c) and weight loss (d) of salmon stored at room temperature. Apparent color change (e) and S value (f) of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 in monitoring salmon freshness, and pH, TVB-N change (g) and weight loss (h) of salmon stored at 4 °C. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TS, EAB, L∗, a, b, ΔE and WS of films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different letters in the same column indicate significantly different (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Gelatin film cross-linked with laccase-catalyzed purple cauliflower extract and coated with Zn-carbon dots exhibits excellent physicochemical properties and functionality for intelligent and active packaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of oxidized purple cauliflower extract (OPE) was conducted by laccase oxidation and served as a reinforcing agent for enhancing physicochemical properties of gelatin (Gel) films. Chitosan (CS) loaded with biowaste-derived Zn-carbon dots (Zn-CD) coating further enhanced the functionality and stability of Gel/OPE film. Structural characterization revealed that OPE and Gel form Schiff base bonds and hydrogen bonds. After the formation of CS/Zn-CD coating, hydrogen bonds formed between CS and Zn-CD, and CS and Gel were closely bonded through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. OPE crosslinking and CS/Zn-CD coating improve mechanical, barrier properties and water resistance of Gel films. CS/Zn-CD coating improved antibacterial and antioxidant ability and illumination stability of composite film, effectively reflected the freshness of salmon during storage. Hence, the functional Gel films developed in this study is hopeful candidate for intelligent and active food packaging application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging is vital for food processing, storage and transportation; prevents microbial contamination; and prolongs the expiration date of foods. As the global population continues to grow and the demand for food safety continues to increase, a scientific strategy for food packaging is imperative. The evolution of consumer lifestyles, characterized by a preference for clean, high quality, fresh, minimally processed and ready-to-eat foods, has increased the demand for innovative packaging solutions. Plastic packaging is mostly made of petroleum. Plastics are inexpensive, durable and malleable. However, conventional petrochemical plastics have severe shortcomings, that include dependence upon the fossil fuels, a large carbon footprint, and lack of degradability (Zhou et al., 2023). Currently, food packaging industry strongly relies heavily on non-degradable oil-based products, comonomers, plasticizers and oligomers for plastic packaging, which may increase food quality as well as security hazards and pollute foods via contamination during food processing (Alamri et al., 2021). With the increasing awareness of environmental protection and the increasing attention to food safety, the use of biodegradable natural materials to prepare film has become a valuable research hotspot in the field of packaging research, especially in the application of food packaging. Hence, there is a great importance to produce green, safe and redevelopable packaging films to replace the unbiodegradable plastic packages (Xia et al., 2023). Most packaging materials originate from natural polymers like proteins, polysaccharides and lipids. For example, Wang, Xie, Tang, Li, et al. (2023) invented an intelligent package that relies on chitosan/fucoidan and anthocyanins for monitoring salmon deterioration.

Gelatin (Gel) is a commonly used protein with favorable film-forming performance, degradability and good biodegradability. Nevertheless, Gel is deficient in bioactivity, and Gel films on their own cannot overcome the damage resulting from microorganisms, UV rays, oxidation, and other such factors present in foodstuffs. Hence, Gel is usually blended with other natural products for enhancing the function for Gel films, and interactions between Gel and phenolic compounds are usually non-covalent, like hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. The relative weakness of these interactions, causing inadequate advancement of Gel films properties, especially their mechanical properties (Bakhshizadeh, Moghaddam, Tavassoli, Ayaseh, & Bangar, 2023; Li et al., 2024; Tang, Zheng, Yang, & Li, 2022). To solve this problem, Ran et al. (2024) used laccase-catalyzed mulberry extract and Pickering emulsions to develop active and intelligent collagen films. After the addition of the laccase-oxidized extract, the internal crosslinking of the film was enhanced, and its mechanical properties improved. However, in this report, collagen extraction was cumbersome, and the production efficiency was low. Commercial Gel is also an animal protein that is inexpensive and easy to obtain and has a structure similar to that of collagen. Obviously, Gel is a better choice. Laccase is an oxidation reductase that directly reduces molecular oxygen to catalyze oxidation of various phenolic compounds, resulting in cross-linking of polyphenol-quinone and the amino group of Gel through Schiff base bonds, improving the microstructure of Gel films and preventing the generation of hydrogen peroxide (Tang et al., 2022). Purple cauliflower is rich in anthocyanins, and purple cauliflower extract (PE) is water soluble and has great antioxidant activity and pH response color change features, cauliflower also contains organic acids with antibacterial activity (Hashim et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2023). Nevertheless, there are no reports on the preparation of intelligent and active packaging films based on the oxidation of purple cauliflower extract catalyzed by laccase (OPE).

Given that Gel is a protein that is exposed to air for a long time and susceptible to the influence of radiation and other factors, it is desirable to design a protective Gel film to reduce its denaturation. Therefore, the application of a polymer coating containing functional nanomaterials on the basis of a single-layer film is useful to improve film performance (Zhang et al., 2022). Chitosan (CS) widely serves as a biopolymer with low cost, good biocompatibility, UV protection and strong film-forming ability (Flórez, Guerra-Rodríguez, Cazón, & Vázquez, 2022). Additionally, CS and Gel can further improve mechanical properties of Gel through hydrogen bonding and hydrostatic interactions between chemically reactive positively charged amino groups (-NH2) of flexible chains of CS and negatively coated carboxyl groups (-COOH) of the side chains of Gel (Li, Tang, & He, 2021). PE will produce a large amount of residue during the extraction process, and discarding or incineration will cause environmental pollution problems. In many studies, plant residue has been converted into a new nanomaterial called green carbon dots (CDs); for example, CDs have been effectively synthesized from purple cabbage residue and banana peel residue (Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Wagh, Khan, Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). CDs are known for their luminescence and biocompatibility (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). CDs have good free radical scavenging activity both in vitro and in vivo and can be used as antioxidants (Bao et al., 2023). However, some research has revealed that CDs have photodynamic antibacterial properties; specifically, the photosensitizer is activated by visible light, causing biochemical reactions that inactivate microorganisms (Chen et al., 2023). However, in the absence of an effective stabilizer, biomass-derived CDs tend to aggregate, which impedes their antioxidant and antimicrobial functions (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024). These factors limit its use in active packaging. An effective method to solve this problem is to use metal nanoparticles to improve the stability and functionality of CDs. CDs have a chemical structure consisting of a central carbon core and a surface incorporated functional group. Physicochemical properties of CDs may vary depending on the chemical structure of the CDs and the specific functional components available as carbon sources (Khan, Ezati, Kim, & Rhim, 2023). To broaden the application range of CDs, new functions can be obtained by adding heteroatoms and metal doping into their core chemical structure. In this regard, ZnO nanoparticle (Zn-NP)-doped CDs have excellent functional properties, and abundant COOH and OH groups on the surface of CDs bind to Zn-NPs, thus stabilizing final structure (Koshy et al., 2022). Chitosan-supported Zn-CD can serve as a functional coating to improve properties of Gel-based films, but no relevant reports on this topic exist.

In this study, oxidized purple cauliflower extract was added to a Gel film to improve its internal crosslinking ability, and CS containing Zn-CD was used as a protective coating. The effects of the OPE concentration and CS/Zn-CD coating on structure, physicochemical properties, antibacterial and antioxidant activities and pH sensitivity of films were investigated. Furthermore, the potential of the prepared films for application in intelligent active packaging was evaluated through monitoring the freshness of salmon.
The materials are described in Section S.1. (Supporting Information).
The preparation and characterization methods of OPE and Zn-CD are described in Section S.1.-S.5. (Supporting Information).

Gel powder (5 g) was diffused in 100 mL of distilled water and heated for 1 h at 50 °C in a water bath with magnetic stirring. Different amounts of OPE solution (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 wt%) and PE solution (8 wt%) were added to 20 g of Gel solution. Mixture was homogenized at 8000 rpm for 5 min to obtain homogeneous solution for film formation. Then, solution was degassed, 20 g film-forming solution was poured into a smooth 9 cm plastic Petri dish and dried under 25 °C for 36 h. The resulting films with different amounts of OPE and PE were named Gel, Gel/OPE2, Gel/OPE4, Gel/OPE6, Gel/OPE8 and Gel/PE8, respectively, these names correspond to the OPE or PE content. The films without OPE serve as the control group.

CS solution was developed by dissolving 1 g of chitosan in 50 mL of 1% acetic acid solution and stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. Zn-CD (3 wt%) was added to CS solution. Solution was stirred vigorously under 40 °C for 2 h, and pour 6 g CS/Zn-CD solution on Gel/OPE8 immediately. The coating was dispersed evenly on Gel/OPE8 using a coating rod. The coating film, which was named CS/CD-Gel/OPE8, was dried at room temperature for 48 h and peeled off. All films were placed under 25 ± 0.5 °C and 75% relative humidity for 48 h and then further measured. The characterization methods and application of films are described in Section S.6. (Supporting Information).
The characterization results of OPE and Zn-CD are described in Section S.8.-S.10. (Supporting Information).

FTIR spectra are utilized to analyze the interactions between components. As shown in Fig. 1 a, Gel absorption peaks appeared at 3308 cm−1 (amide A), 1645 cm−1 (amide I), 1549 cm−1 (amide II) and 1241 cm−1 (amide III), which correspond to O-H and N-H stretching, C=O bending, C-N stretching and N-H bending, and C-N stretching and -CH2 wagging, respectively (Ran et al., 2024). In addition, significant absorption peaks in amides III, II and I were observed spectra of the OPE-modified films, but the peak intensity decreased slightly due to the formation of a C=N bond between the active OPE o-quinone and the amino group of Gel via a Schiff base reaction (Qiang, Chen, Yan, & Liu, 2019). A decrease in peak strength of the amide A band can result from hydrogen bonding between the amino and carboxyl groups of Gel and the free phenolic hydroxyl group of OPE (Li, Zhu, Guan, & Wu, 2019; Zhao et al., 2023). These results indicate that OPE was successfully integrated into the Gel matrix.

XRD patterns of films are detailed in Fig. 1b. A wide diffraction band at approximately 2θ = 22.0° was found for all the films. This corresponds to the amorphous structure of Gel. Li et al. (2023) also reported a similar phenomenon. As OPE content increased, the intensity of the diffraction peak decreased slightly because the interaction between OPE and film matrix attenuated interaction between original polymers. After 8 wt% PE was added, diffraction peak strength of the film lessened significantly, indicating that PE destroyed the inherent crystal structure of Gel and that compatibility of PE with the biopolymer matrix was poor. CS/Zn-CD coating did not significantly affect the diffraction peak intensity or crystal structure of the Gel film, revealing that the surface coating did not cause damage to the original structure of the Gel matrix.

To further confirm the successful cross-linking of Gel with OPE, XPS analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 1c–i, the XPS C 1s spectral peaks of the Gel films are 287.88, 286.28, and 284.88 eV, corresponding to COOH, C-N/C-O and C-H/C-NH2 bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectral peaks are 399.78 and 401.78 eV, which correspond to C-N and C=N bonds, respectively (Ran et al., 2024). After addition of OPE, the relative area of C-H/C-NH2 diminished from 67.50% to 56.87%, and that of C=N bond rose from 2.10% to 3.78%, which was due to the change in relative area caused by the Schiff base reaction between OPE and Gel. Ran et al. (2024) also reported similar discoveries in characterization of oxidized mulberry anthocyanin cross-linking collagen films, in which the relative area of C-H/C-NH2 decreased from 59.09% to 52.85% and that of C=N bond rose from 2.74% to 6.28%.

Properties of films are closely related to its microstructure, and a dense structure enhances mechanical property and barrier property of films (Fan et al., 2023). From Fig. 2 , surface of the pure Gel film was smooth and uniform. As OPE concentration increases, the film surface becomes increasingly dense and uniform due to the good compatibility between OPE and Gel. OPE can be coupled with the chain of a Gel polymer, increasing the availability of the active site and thereby promoting cross-linking between the Gel chains (Ran et al., 2024). Cross-sectional roughness of pure Gel film. As OPE content increased, the crosslinking strength between the Gel and OPE gradually increased, and the cross-sections of the Gel films became increasingly dense. The surface of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was also very smooth and dense, and dense coating structure enhancing barrier performance of the Gel film. Moreover, CS/Zn-CD coating structure can be clearly seen from the cross-section of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8. However, cross-section of the Gel film remained relatively rough after the addition of PE, and large amounts of particle aggregation occurred, indicating poor compatibility between the PE and the film matrix. EDS elemental mapping of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 is shown in Fig. 2. EDS analyzed showed that the major structural elements of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 were carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Additional Zn peaks were found in EDS spectra, verifying successfully integration of Zn-doped carbon dots into polymer matrix.

Apparent viscosity of a film-forming solution reflects the intensity of interactions between components. Generally, the stronger the interaction between components in blends is, the greater the apparent viscosity. Fig. 3 a shows the influence of shear rate on the apparent viscosities of the Gel/PE8 and Gel/OPE8 blend systems. The viscosity of the blend decreases with increasing shear rate, showing a pseudoplastic behavior of the blends. The apparent viscosity of Gel/OPE8 was always greater than that of Gel/PE8 at the same shear rate because of the stronger intermolecular interactions between OPE and Gel, which increased its apparent viscosity. Similarly, Jiang et al. (2023) showed that addition of Oxalis triangularis extract was increased the apparent viscosity of sweet potato starch due to the formation of hydrogen bond between the anthocyanins and sweet potato starch. Therefore, based on the above structural characterization, mechanisms of interactions between components of films may be inferred, Gel chains form Schiff base and hydrogen bond with the OPE, which increases the density of the Gel/OPE8 structure and enhances the adhesion between the interfaces.

Thermal decomposition performance of films was evaluated using TGA. As shown in Fig. 3b, multiple weight losses occur in all films. Thermal decomposition of films was initially noted at approximately 30–130 °C, resulting from the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. The second thermal degradation at approximately 130–290 °C resulted from the degradation of low-molecular-weight proteins in the Gel. The third thermal degradation of the film occurred around 290–600 °C because of decomposition of the high molecular weight Gel (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). The dry matter residues after Gel, Gel/OPE2, Gel/OPE4, Gel/OPE6, Gel/OPE8, Gel/PE8, and CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 treatments at 600 °C were 20.55%, 20.83%, 21.40%, 22.26%, 22.35%, 21.25%, and 26.04%, respectively. Compared with Gel, Gel/OPE8 and CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 had more dry matter remaining treatments at 600 °C were CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 had the most dry matter. Incorporation of OPE and CS/Zn-CD coatings improved the thermal stability of Gel films. Above discoveries were due to the formation of hydrogen bonds, Schiff base bonds and electrostatic interactions among Gel, OPE and CS, which formed cross-linked bonds that made the structure of composite film structure denser and contributed to thermal stability (Fig. 3c).

TS and EAB represent the critical mechanical performance of biopolymer films that determine their stability during storage and transport (Zhao et al., 2023). From Table 1 it can be seen that the TS of functionalized Gel film increased as OPE concentration increased at 76.85 MPa from 25.65 MPa, possibly due to the formation of a micro-network framework and intermolecular forces from OPE crosslinking in the Gel film matrix. The free phenol radicals and/or quinones produced by laccase-catalyzed OPE samples are immediately coupled to the Gel polymer chain, which is arranged as a cross-linked network with improved connectivity (Duan et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the TS of Gel/PE8 was lower than that of Gel/OPE8 due to the more compact microstructure of Gel/OPE8, which was consistent with SEM results. EAB reflects that the films are flexible. EAB values of all films were lower than those of the Gel group, which may be due to steeling effect of OPE benzene ring. Moreover, the enhanced polymer–polymer interaction replaces the weaker oil–polymer interaction with flexibility and high chain mobility, resulting in the structure changing from irregular discontinuity to regular continuity. In addition, altered film framework rearrangement in the presence of OPE (Ran et al., 2024).

WVP is an important property of packaging materials and is associated with the effect of humidity of the environment outside the package on the quality of food inside the package (Wu et al., 2021). WVP of film depends on many factors, which include the macromolecules that make up the film and the degree of cross-linking (Duan et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3d, peak WVP for Gel film was 9.21 × 10−11 g Pa−1 s −1 m−1, whereas peak WVP for the film with OPE was 5.74 × 10−11 g Pa−1·s−1·m−1. This finding is possibly because of the larger aromatic ring and pyridine ring of OPE and the dense microstructure of the film resulting from cross-linking between OPE and film matrix, thus increasing tortuosity of water vapor transfer (Ran et al., 2024). Compared with that of Gel/OPE8, the WVP of Gel/PE8 increased, whereas the WVP of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 decreased because the addition of Zn-CD can occupy the space between chains, form dense network structure, and enhance curvature, hence preventing diffusion of water vapor and increasing the water vapor barrier effect of films (Wu et al., 2024).

OP is a critical index for assessing barrier properties. As shown in Fig. 3e, as the OPE concentration increased, and OP of composite films gradually reduced. However, OP of Gel/PE8 was greater than that of Gel/OPE8, indicating that OPE may improve the oxygen barrier performance of the film significantly. After Zn-CD was added, the OP value was 0.08 g/d·m2, and the OP value reached its lowest value. This could also be because incorporation of Zn-CD added density of the internal structure of surface coating, which complicated the oxygen path in the double layer film. In addition, the forming hydrogen bonds in the polymer matrix can enhance its barrier properties.

WCA embodies hydrophilicity of the composite surface, and Fig. 3f shows WCA data of samples. WCA gradually rose as OPE content increased. However, Gel/PE8 WCA was lower, this was due to the strong interaction between OPE and some hydrophilic groups in Gel film matrix, which reduces affinity of the film surface to water molecules. When CS/Zn-CD coating formed, WCA of the film reached a maximum, which ascribes to a relatively uniform distribution of Zn-CD nanoparticles in film, resulting from formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds with surface active functional groups of the polymer matrix and reduction of hydrophilic groups on surface of film (Li et al., 2023). WCA of Gel/OPE8 and CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 were greater than 80°, revealing their low hydrophilicity. The composite film has excellent moisture resistance and can be utilized under humid environments.

WS of the composite films decreased slowly with addition of OPE, and WS of Gel/PE8 was greater than that of Gel/OPE8 (Table 1). This finding may be attributed to the fact that phenolic hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in OPE have many binding sites that may couple with polar groups of collagen, thus reducing affinity of water molecules. CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 has the lowest WS, which can result from the uniform distribution of Zn-CD in composite film, causing formation of hydrogen bonds between Zn-CD and the biopolymer, and amount of free hydrophilic groups is effectively reduced (Li et al., 2023).

Films were laid on school emblem to take photos, as displayed in Fig. 4 a. All the films show outstanding transparency. OPE addition did not tremendously alter the film appearance. Yellow color of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was because of brownish-yellow color of Zn-CD. Red color of Gel/PE8 can be attributed to the inherent color of PE. Color parameters (a, b, L∗ and ΔE) of films are detailed as Table 1. L∗ denotes parameter alteration from black to white, indicates parameter alteration from green to red, b denotes parameter change from blue to yellow, and ΔE indicates total color difference (Jiang et al., 2022). As OPE concentration increased, L∗ value decreased from 91.35 to 71.44, revealing that lightness of composite films decreased. Here, a did not change tremendously, and b increased from −3.47 ± 0.56 to 5.65 ± 0.45, indicating that the composite film gradually turned yellow after the addition of OPE. In addition, ΔE increased from 8.21 ± 0.12 to 26.54 ± 1.19 with increasing OPE concentration. Compared with those of other films, a, b, L∗ and ΔE values of Gel/PE8 change significantly, potentially due to the effect of anthocyanins on film color. Wang, Xie, Tang, Hao, et al. (2023) also stated a similar phenomenon occurs while eggplant anthocyanins are added to chitosan/chitin nanofiber films. Compared with Gel/OPE8, the addition of the CS/Zn-CD coating reduced L∗ value of film and increased a, b and ΔE values, resulting from the inherent color of CS and Zn-CD. Color parameters of composite films match their physical appearance.

pH sensitivity of the films was tested within different buffers (pH 3 to 11) for assessing potential utility of films as indicator of freshness. From Fig. 4b, Gel film color never altered tremendously under diverse pH environment, revealing Gel film did not own pH response capability, which matched observation of Khan, Riahi, Kim, and Rhim (2023). In contrast, OPE-modified films exhibit a pH-dependent color shift from red to light purple to dark blue and finally yellow, and these color changes may be associated with the pH response of the OPE. When the OPE concentration increases, color of composite film is deepened, but color of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 composite becomes slightly lighter than that of the Gel/OPE8 composite. This finding may be due to the superposition of the CS/Zn-CD coating, which interferes with the color appearance to a certain extent but does not strongly influence the overall change in film color.

Fig. 4c displays UV–vis spectra of different films. Gel exhibited good light transmittance above 400 nm. However, at UV wavelengths below 400 nm, especially at 200–290 nm, the transmittance is low, indicating that Gel has a certain anti-ultraviolet ability. When the OPE concentration increased from 0 to 8 wt%, the light resistance of the functionalized Gel film in the visible and UV bands increase (Ran et al., 2024). Although light transmittance of Gel film in visible region declined after the addition of PE, the light transmittance at 200–280 nm increased. However, the addition of carbon dots almost completely blocks UV-B-ray transmittance (CS/CD-Gel/OPE8), revealing addition of Zn-CD can provide the film with comprehensive UV protection at slightest sacrifice of transparency.

The DPPH and ABTS methods were used for evaluating antioxidant activity of films, and results were detailed as Fig. 4d and e. With the increase of OPE, scavenging ability of ABTS and DPPH free radicals gradually increases. However, compared with Gel/OPE8, Gel/PE8 has stronger antioxidant activity, potentially resulting from the existence of glycosylation, phenol ring double bonds and hydroxyl side chains. In addition, anthocyanins exhibit strong antioxidant activity in vitro and are thus able to scavenge free radicals (Ran et al., 2024). However, CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 has the highest oxidation resistance, which can result from the presence of multiple reducing electron donor groups on the surface of Zn-CD that directly reacting with free radicals and serve as antioxidants (Li et al., 2023), as well as presence of phenolic ring double bonds, hydroxyl side chains and glycosylation, enabling scavenging of free radicals (Teng et al., 2017). CS/Zn-CD coating compensates for the lack of the weak oxidation resistance of the OPE films. Compared with the DPPH method, the ABTS method has a greater antioxidant capacity given the good interaction between the ABTS aqueous solution and hydrophilic Zn-CD. The packaging material has high antioxidant properties and can serve as an antioxidant packaging material for preventing quality degradation caused by oxidation during storage.

From Fig. 4f and g, as OPE content increased, diameter of the antibacterial zone slowly increased because OPE contains numerous free phenolic hydroxyl groups with powerful reductive properties. These properties can destroy secondary structure of protein molecules and influence normal development and metabolism of bacteria, leading to apoptosis (Ran et al., 2024). The antibacterial effect of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was the strongest among the other tested films. This was because both CS and Zn-CD have antibacterial abilities, and CS and Zn-CD synergistically enhance the antibacterial ability of Gel/OPE8.

Color stability of the film is very important for colorimetric applications. Fig. S4a (Supporting Information) shows the color changes of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 and Gel/OPE8 under visible light for 21 days under room temperature. In the preservation process, color of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was well preserved, and that of Gel/OPE8 was slightly yellow. The relative color difference (S values) was used as a measure of the degree of discoloration of film. As shown in Fig. S4c (Supporting Information), compared with that of Gel/OPE8, S value of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 increases more slowly under visible light. At the end of storage, the S value of the Gel/OPE8 film increased to 3.17%. The S value of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was only 2.49%. This phenomenon was attributable mainly to i) being consistent with the resistance of zinc oxide to light and oxygen and zinc oxide protecting oxidized anthocyanins from degradation and ii) added to CS/Zn-CD coating increasing light resistance of Gel/OPE8. Fig. S4b (Supporting Information) shows color changes of the two films following 32 h of ultraviolet irradiation. The color of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was relatively stable during storage. After 32 h storage (Fig. S4d in Supporting Information), S value of the Gel/OPE8 films rose to 3.07%, and the S value of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was 2.78%, which can be attributed to the following: i) Zn-CD UV absorption capacity, as previously mentioned; ii) chitosan has high UV protection properties (Sul et al., 2023). Thus, the CS/Zn-CD coating effectively protects Gel/OPE8 from UV-induced photooxidation and photodegradation reactions. Figs. S4e–f (Supporting Information) shows the FTIR spectra of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 and Gel/OPE8 after 21 days and 32 h of UV irradiation, respectively. From Figs. S4e–f (Supporting Information), the film did not form new peaks or generate new chemical bonds, indicating that light has little impact on chemical structure of film. Cause of the color change may be degradation of anthocyanins or oxidation of film matrix.

Accorded to the above experimental discoveries, CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 exhibits better comprehensive performance, so it is used for monitoring freshness of salmon at room temperature and 4 °C. Protein-rich foods release TVB-N during spoilage process, which alters pH level of the salmon surface and causes a change in colorimetric film color. Color and S value of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 at room temperature and the pH, TVB-N and weight loss of salmon storage period are detailed in Fig. 5 a–d. The initial pH and TVB-N of fresh salmon at room temperature were 6.38 ± 0.01 and 6.25 ± 0.93 mg/100 g, respectively. On the first day, the color of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 gradually changed to light pink. The pH of the salmon was 6.43 ± 0.02, and TVB-N reached 13.77 ± 0.82 mg/100 g. The above discoveries resulted from large number of microorganisms on salmon surface, which broke down proteins to generate TVB-N. This leads to a change in surface pH of salmon, which changed the color of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8. The weight loss was 1.57 ± 0.09%, which can result from oxidative consumption, bacterial breakdown and water loss in salmon (Wang, Xie, Tang, Li, et al., 2023). On Day 2, under microbial action, TVB-N value reached 30.81 ± 0.87 mg/100 g. Based on the national standard GB/T 18108-2019, the limit of TVB-N in salmon must to be less than 30 mg/100 g (Wang, Xie, Tang, Li, et al., 2023), revealing that salmon deteriorated on the second day. In addition, the pH and weight loss rate continued to increase. The color of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 deepened, and the pH and S increased, reaching 6.74 ± 0.02 and 3.17 ± 0.11%, respectively. On Day 3, with further salmon deterioration, TVB-N reached 57.73 ± 0.97 mg/100 g, the salmon were completely disrupted, and the pH was 7.12 ± 0.01. At this time, the color of CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 changed to pale purple because the microorganisms multiplying on the surface of the salmon consumed a large amount of dry matter, causing a change in environmental pH (Wang, Xie, Tang, Hao, et al., 2023). Weight loss also increased, and salmon were no longer suitable for Chinese consumption standards. Similar changes in color, S value, TVB-N and pH were observed while salmon were stored at 4 °C (Fig. 5e–h). On the 12th day, TVB-N concentration reached 38.6 ± 0.56 mg/100 g, and the weight loss was 9.16 ± 0.31%. In addition, the pH was 8.05 ± 0.03, and the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 color was light blue. Moreover, the S value was 17.49 ± 0.38%, and the label color clearly changed. However, salmon appearance did not change tremendously throughout the experiment, and it was difficult for consumers to discern freshness by eye alone. The color of the CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 changed from the original color to purple to purple/blue as the freshness of the salmon changed. In summary, CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 is an excellent indicator of salmon freshness.

In the research, functionalized Gel films on basis of OPE and CS/Zn-CD coating were prepared and characterized. The microstructures of the OPE-modified Gel films were denser, and the resulting films exhibited better light resistance, mechanical and barrier properties and pH color responsiveness. Because of the excellent antioxidant, antibacterial and UV protection properties of Zn-CD, the use of CS/Zn-CD as a coating for active packaging can significantly increase the functionality of polymer films. CS/CD-Gel/OPE8 was used for salmon freshness monitoring, which can determine salmon freshness according to changes in film color. Our results show that CS/CD-Gel/OPE8, which has excellent antibacterial, antioxidant and pH sensitivity, as well as improved mechanical and barrier properties, shows great potential as an active intelligent package. Moreover, the use of a CS/Zn-CD coating may represent a powerful strategy for property improvement on polymer films. In the future, effectiveness of functional active packaging with a CS/Zn‒CD coating for food preservation needs to be further studied.

Yanyan Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Longwei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tiantian Liu: Data curation. Mei Li: Software. Ziao Li: Investigation. Yingzhu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Anhui Agricultural University talent introduction project (rc352307), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022MD723756) and the open project of the Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Engineering in universities of the Education Department of Autonomous Region (TDNG2023101).
